title: Antibiotics & painkillers — medicines that help
examTip: AQA regularly asks why antibiotics don't work on viruses, and why resistance is increasing. Know the natural selection mechanism cold — mutation, survival, reproduction, spread — and always finish your answer with why that's a problem for treatment.
introTips: This lesson is about two very different types of medicine: antibiotics (which kill bacteria) and painkillers (which treat symptoms).
The antibiotic resistance section is a favourite in exams — it follows the same natural selection logic you learned in B4.
You'll also understand why your doctor tells you to finish the whole course of antibiotics, not just stop when you feel better.
intro: When you're ill, there are two things medicine can do: attack the cause, or relieve how you feel. Antibiotics are designed to attack the cause — but only when bacteria are the enemy. Understanding the difference between bacterial and viral infections isn't just biology trivia. It explains one of the biggest medical crises of the 21st century: the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

keyPoints.heading: What antibiotics do — and don't do
keyPoints.content: An antibiotic is a medicine that either kills bacteria or stops bacteria reproducing. Different antibiotics target different types of bacteria — for example, penicillin targets certain bacterial cell wall structures. Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses. Viruses are not cells — they have no cell wall, no metabolism of their own, and they reproduce by hijacking your cells. There is no antibiotic that can target them without harming you at the same time. This is why you don't get antibiotics for a cold or flu. Viral infections are treated with antiviral drugs instead, which work on different principles — or your immune system deals with them on its own.
writeBullets: Antibiotics kill bacteria OR stop them reproducing
Different antibiotics target different bacteria — they are specific
Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses — viruses are not cells
Viral infections use antiviral drugs, or the immune system fights them
Example: penicillin targets the bacterial cell wall structure

keyTerms.def: A medicine that kills bacteria or inhibits their reproduction — does NOT work on viruses
A medicine that targets viruses specifically — a different class of drug from antibiotics
A microorganism that causes disease — includes bacteria, viruses, fungi and protists

keyPoints.memTip: Antibiotics = anti-BACTERIA. The clue is in the name. If someone asks 'why can't antibiotics treat a cold?', your answer has three parts: 1) colds are caused by viruses, 2) viruses are not cells, 3) antibiotics have nothing to target. Say all three and you'll get full marks.

keyPoints.heading: Antibiotic resistance — how bacteria fight back
keyPoints.content: Here's something alarming: bacteria are evolving faster than we can make new antibiotics. This is antibiotic resistance, and it happens through natural selection. In any large population of bacteria, there will be random mutations — small changes in DNA. Some of those mutations, by chance, make a bacterium slightly harder for an antibiotic to kill. When antibiotics are used, most bacteria die — but the resistant ones survive. They reproduce rapidly (bacteria can divide every 20 minutes), passing the resistance gene on. Over time, the whole population becomes resistant. MRSA (meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is a famous example — it's resistant to many antibiotics and is very dangerous in hospitals. Overuse and misuse of antibiotics drives this process: using them for viral infections (where they do nothing) or not finishing a course puts selective pressure on bacteria without wiping them out.
writeBullets: Random mutation in a bacterium → happens to confer antibiotic resistance
Antibiotic kills non-resistant bacteria; resistant bacterium survives
Resistant bacterium reproduces — passes resistance gene to offspring
Resistance spreads through the population
MRSA = meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — resistant to many antibiotics
Overuse and misuse (e.g. not finishing a course) speeds up resistance

keyTerms.def: When bacteria develop the ability to survive exposure to an antibiotic, making that drug ineffective
The process by which organisms with advantageous traits survive and reproduce more, passing those traits on
Meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — a bacterium resistant to many common antibiotics; a serious hospital infection risk
A random change in the DNA sequence of an organism — can occasionally provide an advantage like antibiotic resistance

keyPoints.memTip: Spell out the natural selection story every time: Mutation → Survival → Reproduction → Spread. Four steps. AQA mark schemes often have one mark for each step. If you write 'bacteria become immune' you'll lose marks — bacteria don't choose to resist. It's random mutation that gets selected for. Accuracy matters here.

keyPoints.heading: Why you must finish the course
keyPoints.content: When a doctor prescribes a 7-day course of antibiotics, they mean all 7 days — even if you feel completely better on day 4. Here is why. Early in the course, the least resistant bacteria die first — they're easiest to kill. As the course continues, more resistant individuals are eliminated. If you stop early, you've killed the weakest bacteria and left alive the ones that were hardest to kill — the most resistant ones. Those survivors reproduce and the next infection is caused by a more resistant population. A full course ensures the entire bacterial population is eliminated, giving resistant individuals no chance to reproduce and spread.
writeBullets: Antibiotics kill the least resistant bacteria first
Stopping early leaves the most resistant bacteria alive
Survivors reproduce — next infection is harder to treat
Completing the full course eliminates the entire bacterial population
This prevents resistant strains from establishing and spreading

keyTerms.def: A prescribed number of days of antibiotic treatment designed to fully eliminate a bacterial infection
An environmental factor (like an antibiotic) that favours the survival of some organisms over others, driving natural selection

keyPoints.memTip: Think of it like weeding a garden. If you only pull up the easy weeds and leave the tough ones, the tough ones take over. Finishing the course = pulling every weed. Stopping early = giving the toughest weeds a head start. Use that image to explain the idea in the exam and it'll stick.

keyPoints.heading: Painkillers — treating symptoms, not causes
keyPoints.content: Painkillers such as ibuprofen and paracetamol do not kill pathogens and do not treat infections. What they do is relieve symptoms — they reduce pain, fever, and inflammation. This is still genuinely useful: reducing fever makes you more comfortable and can prevent complications; reducing pain allows you to rest and recover. Painkillers are valuable alongside other treatments — you might take paracetamol to manage a fever while your immune system fights off a viral infection. The key exam point is the distinction: antibiotics treat the cause (bacteria), painkillers treat the symptoms. Neither type of drug is 'better' — they do different things.
writeBullets: Painkillers (e.g. ibuprofen, paracetamol) relieve symptoms — pain, fever, inflammation
They do NOT kill pathogens and do NOT cure infections
Still useful: reduce fever, allow rest, make recovery more comfortable
Antibiotics treat the cause (bacteria); painkillers treat symptoms only
Both types have a role — they do different jobs

keyTerms.def: A drug that relieves pain and other symptoms without treating the underlying infection or killing pathogens
An anti-inflammatory painkiller that reduces pain, fever and swelling
A common painkiller and fever reducer — does not treat infection
A sign or feeling caused by a disease, such as pain, fever, or a rash — not the disease itself

keyPoints.memTip: Cause vs symptom — that's the whole distinction. Antibiotics attack the cause. Painkillers attack the symptoms. If an exam question asks 'why are painkillers useful even though they don't cure infection?', your answer is: they reduce symptoms like pain and fever, making recovery easier and preventing complications.

1.question: Why do antibiotics have no effect on a viral infection like influenza (flu)?
options: Viruses are too small for antibiotics to reach them
Viruses are not cells — they have no cell wall or metabolism for the antibiotic to target
Antibiotics only work if taken before symptoms appear
Viruses quickly become resistant to antibiotics
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right — viruses aren't cells, so antibiotics have nothing to attack. You need antiviral drugs or your own immune system to deal with a virus.
1.wrongFeedback: The key reason is that viruses are not cells. Antibiotics work by targeting bacterial structures — like cell walls or bacterial enzymes. Viruses don't have these, so antibiotics can't do anything to them.

2.question: A patient stops taking their antibiotic course after 4 days because they feel better. What is the most likely consequence?
options: The infection will clear on its own as their immune system takes over
The most resistant bacteria have survived and may reproduce, making future infection harder to treat
The remaining bacteria will not become resistant because they weren't exposed to the antibiotic
The antibiotic will continue working for several days after the last dose
2.correctFeedback: Correct — stopping early leaves the most resistant bacteria alive. They reproduce and pass on resistance genes, making the next infection harder to treat.
2.wrongFeedback: Stopping early is a problem because it leaves the hardest-to-kill bacteria alive. Those survivors reproduce, and the resistance genes spread. Always complete the full course to eliminate the entire bacterial population.

commonMistakes: Writing 'antibiotics kill viruses too' or 'antibiotics treat infections' without specifying bacterial infections — antibiotics only target bacteria, never viruses.
Saying bacteria 'become immune' to antibiotics — bacteria do not develop immunity. Resistance arises through random mutation and natural selection, not a deliberate response.
Leaving out the reproduction step in the natural selection answer — the resistant bacterium must reproduce and pass on the gene. Surviving alone is not enough.
Confusing painkillers with antibiotics — painkillers do not kill any pathogens. They only relieve symptoms.
Thinking MRSA is a new type of bacteria — MRSA is Staphylococcus aureus that has evolved resistance through natural selection. It's the same species, with an added advantage.
examTips: For antibiotic resistance questions, always write the four-step natural selection chain: random mutation → resistant bacterium survives antibiotic → reproduces → resistance spreads through population. Each step can carry a mark.

If asked to evaluate the claim 'we should stop prescribing antibiotics for minor infections', use your knowledge of resistance: agree that overuse drives resistance, so reserving antibiotics for serious infections slows down the process.
Distinguish carefully between 'treating an infection' (killing the pathogen) and 'treating symptoms' (relieving discomfort). Examiners will mark wrong if you say painkillers treat infections.

revisionCardBullets: Antibiotics: kill bacteria or stop reproduction — do NOT work on viruses
Different antibiotics target different bacteria — specificity matters
Viruses are not cells — no cell wall or metabolism for antibiotics to target
Resistance mechanism: random mutation → survives antibiotic → reproduces → spreads
MRSA = meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — resistant to many antibiotics
Overuse and misuse (viral infections, not finishing courses) drives resistance
Finish the course: incomplete courses leave the most resistant bacteria alive to reproduce
Painkillers (ibuprofen, paracetamol): relieve symptoms only — do not kill pathogens
Antibiotics treat the cause; painkillers treat symptoms — different roles, both useful
